segs.utf8:
Welcome to Real Easy English from 
BBC Learning English. I'm Beth.

And I'm Neil. In this podcast, we have 
a real conversation in easy English.

If you want to read along, you can visit 
our website. BBC Learning English dot com.

— Hi, Neil.
— Hi, Beth. How are you?
I'm very good, thank you. How are you?

— I'm pretty good, yes.
— Good, good.
What are we talking about today?
Well, today we are talking about food.

In particular,
we'll talk about our favourite foods
and how often we eat them.

What an amazing topic.
I have so much to say about food.
But you said how often.

What do we mean by
how often we eat something?
Mm, good question!

'How often' means 'how many times'
we eat our favourite food.

So we might eat them every week,
every day, or if we really like a food,

we might eat it for breakfast,
lunch and dinner — every meal!
So, Neil, what is your favourite food?
Well, that's a really difficult question,
but I really like fish.
Ah, me too!

I was gonna say that, but actually,
I really love seafood.

So, I think I’d say any kind of dish
with seafood in it,
I absolutely love.
So, how often do you eat seafood, Beth?
Well, not very often, actually.

I have it when I go on holiday
and I have it in restaurants sometimes,
but I don't really cook seafood at home.

What about you?
How often do you have fish?
Oh, probably a few times a week.

— Really?
— Maybe, yeah.
Yeah. We eat a lot of fish.

We sometimes have fish at home.
I normally have it once or twice a week.
Right.

But I never have seafood at home,
actually.

Yeah, OK.
So what is your least favourite food?
Ooh, I absolutely hate baked beans!
You hate baked beans!?
I think they're disgusting!
I can't even...

The smell of them
even makes me feel a bit sick.
Oh, OK.

So we've talked about
the foods that we really like
and the foods that we don't like.

But in a general week,
what do you normally cook?
Often, we'll eat Indian curries,

but also Italian pasta dishes
or Spanish dishes

and then sometimes more traditional
British roast dinner kind of thing.
Yeah.

I think, generally,
we have the same at home.

Like, we eat a lot of food
from different countries.

But I have to say I usually cook
quite a lot of pasta

and quite a lot of rice at home
in general,
just because they cook really quickly!

So, Beth. You generally eat
rice and pasta dishes.
What does that mean, 'generally'?

Well, we use 'generally'
and 'usually' and 'normally'
for habits or things that we do a lot.

So, for example,
if I generally cook pasta or rice,

that means maybe five times out
of the seven in a week, I would say.
OK, so that’s most of the time.

If we use 'sometimes' we mean
that we'd do it less than 'generally'.

Yeah, sometimes
might be two or three times a week.

I guess it depends on
what you're talking about.

But for example, with me,
I sometimes cook fish.

Like I said I have it
maybe twice a week.
So I would say that’s sometimes.
Beth, you said that you hate baked beans.
So do you ever eat baked beans?
Never. No, I can't stand them.
So I never, ever have baked beans.

Well, 'never'
is something that we don't do.
We just don't ever do it.

I can’t believe
you don't like baked beans.
Beans on toast is a classic!
It is. But, no. Not for me.

Let's quickly recap the vocabulary
we learnt during the conversation.

We learnt 'often'
which means 'we do something a lot'.
And we learnt 'how often',

which we use to ask someone
'how many times they do something'.
'Usually', 'normally', and 'generally',

which are all ways to talk about
'things we do a lot'.

'Sometimes',
which we can use

to talk about 'things we do,
but not always or often'.

And 'never' to talk about 'things
we don't do', like eat beans, Beth.

Yes, just like I never eat baked beans.
Urgh, horrible!

Thanks for listening to Real Easy 
English. Visit our website for

more activities and courses to help you with 
your English. BBC Learning English dot com.
Next week, we'll talk about our friends.
See you then. Goodbye!
Bye!
